story:
Elias Veyra grew up in a house where justice was only a word. His father, a high-court judge praised in public, was monstrous at home. He mixed mockery with fists, paraded his power, and once made Elias watch a beggar plead in court, then laughed as he signed the man’s imprisonment. Elias swore he would never become him.

Then came Anna, a girl with broken-glass eyes and a stepfamily that treated her like a stain. Her mother, a nightclub singer, had died too young; her stepfather resented her; her stepmother dismissed her with a shrug. At university, Elias and Anna found each other, clung like lifelines, and built a secret world where no gavel, no cruel hand, could reach.

For a while, hope held. They dreamed of marriage, of children raised far from houses of hate. But monsters do not release their children easily. One summer night after a gala, attackers set upon them. Elias was beaten and shot in the back, left bleeding on the curb, while Anna was dragged into an alley and violated by the arrogant son of a tycoon, a boy untouchable by law. The trial was a farce. His father presided, smirked, and dismissed the case. Wealth paved the boy’s invincibility. That day, Anna broke. Elias followed. They wept, raged, and, in their grief, they planned. If the world worshipped monsters, they told themselves, they would become worse.

Elias began with his father. He poisoned the whiskey the man had once forced him to sip as a boy. He watched the judge writhe and choke on the floor. “Like Mother,” he whispered—what he believed, rightly or not, that his father had done—“but slower.” Satisfaction flickered, then something colder took root: the knowledge that he had crossed a line he could never uncross.

Anna turned to her stepmother. She lured her to the old nightclub and set the curtains ablaze, then stood in the alley as smoke curled and screams rose, telling herself, “For her,” as she clutched Elias’s hand. Weeks later, her stepfather drowned in what tabloids called a yacht mishap. In truth, Anna held his head under while Elias steered farther out to sea, both of them shaking when it was done. And the boy who had ruined her? His car sailed off a bridge at midnight. Investigators called it drunk driving. No one traced the cut brakes back to Elias.

The city mourned its sudden tragedies. Power shifted. Rivals took blame. The press devoured scandal and missed the quiet couple walking untouched through the wreckage. Elias and Anna moved into his father’s mansion. In rooms that felt smaller without the monster, his stepmother—too naïve to suspect—welcomed Anna warmly. Time passed. They graduated. Elias slid into his father’s robes. The gavel was heavy, but it fit. Outside, the world whispered justice. Inside, he knew the truth: he had become the shadow he’d tried to escape. He told himself monsters were the only ones who ever won, knowing the cost of that belief.

Anna became pregnant. Fear trailed every heartbeat until their daughter arrived—alive, breathing, beautiful. They did not know how to be perfect parents; they knew only what not to do. No fists. No cruelty. No silence where love should be. At night, when the baby cried, Elias sometimes saw his father’s stern eyes in the window and hated the reflection. Then Anna’s arms would circle him, their daughter’s giggle would shatter the fear, and, for a moment, the past loosened its grip.

They had killed, burned, drowned, and lied. Justice had failed them; what they chose next carried its own ruin. Yet they lived on, survivors cloaked in normalcy, their love forged in vengeance and tempered—imperfectly—by the vow to end the cycle. No one knew the truth but them. And as they smiled at the life they had carved, they understood: they had become monsters, yes—but monsters who would not pass the monster on.
